title: Growing tomatoes from seed
subtitle: Indoors in late February, on the windowsill through spring, in the ground or pot by the end of May.
excerpt: Tomatoes grow from seed sown indoors in late February to early March. Pricked-out seedlings spend two months on a south-facing windowsill, harden off in May, and crop from July to the first frost. The full UK calendar in one tutorial.
sourceNotes:
Cultural notes drawn from two references:

• Royal Horticultural Society, tomato growing guide (rhs.org.uk/vegetables/tomatoes) for the UK sowing window and the hardening-off cadence.
• Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), §The kitchen garden. Project Gutenberg #14443. Public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: Lifting seedlings from a crowded seed tray into individual pots once they have their first pair of true leaves. The seedling is held by a leaf (never the stem) while the roots are eased from the compost.

The second and subsequent pairs of leaves on a seedling, shaped like the adult plant's leaves. The first leaves to appear are seed leaves (cotyledons), which look different and feed the seedling for its first week or two.
Acclimatising a seedling to outdoor conditions over a week or two by putting it outside for progressively longer periods. Skipping this step kills the seedling on its first cold night out.

A tomato grown as a single tall stem, with side-shoots removed weekly. Cordons need a stake; they crop heavily on a small footprint. The opposite is a bush tomato, which sprawls and crops without staking.

content.text: A tomato from seed is a four-month project that splits across two locations. The first two months happen indoors on a sunny windowsill where seeds sprout, grow their first true leaves, and become small plants. The last two months happen outdoors where the plants set fruit, ripen through July and August, and crop until the first frost. The UK calendar is tight: sow in late February or early March; plant out at the end of May; the last fruit ripens (or comes in green to ripen on a windowsill) in early October.

Tomato seed itself is cheap (a packet has more seed than a household needs for a single year). The work and time investment is on the seedling side: keeping the indoor seedlings on a south-facing windowsill, turning them daily, watering them carefully, and then hardening them off properly so they survive the transplant outdoors.
What you need

Tomato seed in a variety suited to the UK climate. "Gardener's Delight" (small red cherry, reliable, cordon), "Sungold" (small orange cherry, sweet, cordon), or "Roma" (paste tomato, bush) are good first choices. One small seed packet contains 20 to 40 seeds; a household of two only needs five or six plants.

Seed compost (or general multi-purpose compost sieved to remove the lumps). A small seed tray or six 9 cm pots for the first sowing. Larger 9 cm pots for pricking out. A south-facing windowsill or a heated propagator. Garden canes or bamboo stakes (one per cordon plant). A patch of sunny outdoor ground or a 30 cm container per plant. General-purpose fertiliser or tomato feed for the cropping months.
Late February to early March: sowing

Fill a seed tray or pots with seed compost and firm the surface lightly. Water from below by sitting the tray in a tray of water for ten minutes; tomato seed dislikes being top-watered before germination.
Scatter the seed thinly on the compost surface, then cover with a thin layer of compost or vermiculite (about the depth of the seed itself). Don't bury them deeply.
Put the tray on a south-facing windowsill or in a heated propagator at about 20 °C. Germination takes seven to fourteen days. Keep the compost just damp.
Seedlings appear with two narrow seed-leaves first. The actual tomato-shaped leaves (the
true leaves
) come a week or two later.
Mid-March: pricking out
When seedlings have one full pair of true leaves,
prick them out

into individual 9 cm pots. Use a dibber (or a chopstick) to ease each seedling out of the seed tray; hold the seedling by a seed leaf, never the stem. Drop it into a pot of fresh compost with the lower half of the stem buried (tomatoes root from the buried stem, giving a stronger plant). Water in and return to the windowsill.
April to mid-May: growing on

Keep the pots on the sunniest windowsill in the house. Turn the pots a quarter-turn every day so the seedlings grow upright instead of leaning towards the light. Water when the compost surface feels dry, never letting the pots stand in water. Pot on to a slightly larger pot if the roots fill the 9 cm pot before transplant day.
Mid-May: hardening off
Once the risk of frost has passed (mid-May in southern England, late May in northern England and Scotland), the plants need
hardening off
Days 1 to 2: put the pots outside in a sheltered spot for two hours during the warmest part of the afternoon; bring them in before dusk.
Days 3 to 5: leave them out for half a day, sheltered from strong wind.
Days 6 to 8: leave them out all day, bring in overnight.
Days 9 to 10: leave them out day and night unless frost is forecast.
End of May: planting out
Plant out at the end of May or first week of June, after the hardening-off run. For
cordon

varieties, drive a cane next to the planting hole first and tie the plant loosely to the cane. Space cordons 45 cm apart; bush varieties 60 cm apart. Container plants want one plant per 30 cm pot. Water in well.
June to October: cropping

Cordon tomatoes need a weekly side-shoot pass: pinch out the shoots that appear in the angle between the stem and each leaf-stalk. The plant puts its energy into fruit on the main stem. Tie the stem to the cane every 15 cm or so as the plant grows. Once the plant has set six or seven trusses of fruit, pinch out the growing tip; the existing fruit will ripen, and the plant won't waste energy on flowers that wouldn't finish before the autumn.

Water consistently (irregular watering causes blossom-end rot and split skins) and feed weekly with a high-potash tomato feed once the first truss has set. Pick fruit as it ripens; any green tomatoes left at the end of the season ripen on a sunny windowsill or go into green tomato chutney.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first tomato year
attrs.intro: Five mistakes that most new tomato growers make in their first attempt.

items.symptom: Seedlings are tall, pale, and lean towards the window
items.cause: Not enough light. Tomato seedlings on a north-facing windowsill or in dim corners stretch towards what light there is.
items.fix: Move to the sunniest windowsill in the house. Turn the pots daily. If the leaning is severe, the plants can be re-potted deeper to bury the stretched stem, and they will recover.

items.symptom: Seedlings flop over and die overnight
items.cause: Damping-off, a fungal disease caused by wet compost in cold conditions.
items.fix: Water from below, not above. Don't sow until the room is reliably 18 to 20 °C. Avoid overwatering; the compost should be damp, not wet. Once damping-off is seen, the affected pot is usually a write-off; sow fresh seed in clean compost.

items.symptom: Hardening off was skipped; the plants wilted and died on the first night out
items.cause: Sudden temperature shock from windowsill (20 °C) to garden (8 °C overnight).
items.fix: Sow a second batch; the late-sown plants catch up if the season is reasonable. Don't skip hardening off on the replacements.

items.symptom: Brown patches develop on the bottom of the fruit (blossom-end rot)
items.cause: Irregular watering during fruit development, which prevents the plant from moving calcium into the developing fruit.
items.fix: Water consistently. Affected fruit can't be saved; remove them so the plant doesn't waste energy. New fruit will set normally once watering steadies.

items.symptom: By September, fruit is still green and not ripening
items.cause: Cool autumn weather, common in northern England and Scotland.
items.fix: Pick the green fruit and ripen indoors on a windowsill alongside a banana (the ethylene helps), or make green tomato chutney with the remainder. Don't leave them on the plant past the first frost.

content.text: Where this goes next

A productive tomato year produces more fruit than a household eats fresh. The traditional next steps are preserving: passata or oven-roasted tomatoes for the freezer, tomato chutney for the cupboard, or sun-dried tomatoes if you can sustain the oven temperature long enough. Save seed from one ripe fruit of a non-F1 variety for sowing the year after: the rinsed-and-dried seed keeps for four or five years in a labelled envelope.
